paragraphs: M ANAGERS AND THE  C ULTURAL  T RANSMISSION OF G ENDER  N ORMS * Virginia Minni † Kieu-Trang Nguyen ‡ Heather Sarsons § July 31, 2024

sections.section_name: Click here for the latest version
paragraphs: P RELIMINARY . P LEASE DO NOT CIRCULATE .

Abstract This paper examines the influence of managers’ gender norms on gender dispari- ties in labor market outcomes. Using data from a multinational operating in over 100 countries, we exploit cross-country manager rotations that are orthogonal to workers to estimate the impact of male managers’ gender attitudes on the work outcomes of male and female workers within the same team. We find that managers from coun- tries with 1s.d. more progressive gender attitudes narrow the gender pay gap by 3.8 percent, primarily by promoting women at higher rates. The effects last beyond the manager’s rotation and are three times larger when the manager is assigned to coun- tries with conservative gender attitudes. Moreover, workers in the destination office change their own attitudes, as evidenced by those workers in turn being more gender- equal with their subordinates. Our evidence points to individual managers as critical in shaping corporate culture. Keywords: MNEs, managers, gender gaps, corporate culture JEL:  J16, J24, J31, F23, M14 * We are particularly grateful to the global learning team and the data analytics team at the multinational for sharing the data and invaluable context knowledge. We thank Abhijit Tagade and Kevin Wong for great research assistance. This manuscript was not subject to prior review by any party, except to verify that it does not contain any confidential data or information, as per the research collaboration contract signed at the outset of this project. † University of Chicago, Booth School of Business, CEPR and IZA. virginia.minni@chicagobooth.edu ‡ Kellogg School of Management. kieu-trang.nguyen@kellogg.northwestern.edu § Vancouver School of Economics, University of British Columbia. hsarsons@mail.ubc.ca

Multinationals employ an increasingly large share of workers in developing countries, where gender norms and attitudes tend to be less progressive. At the same time, em- ployees within a multinational are exposed not only to the headquarter’s policies and norms (e.g., Hjort, Li, and Sarsons, 2022) but also managers from a range of cultural back- grounds, who likely bring along their own cultural views including their gender atti- tudes. Those cross-country interactions suggest that multinationals could have a role in transmisting more progressive gender norms to their foreign establishments, consistent with a plethora of anecdotal evidence and macro-level correlations. 1 On the other hand, there is little rigorous empirical evidence of the existence and impacts of this type of cul- tural transmisison within firm, partly due to the dearth of appropiate settings, data, and measures to study this question. Thus this remains an important inquiry, with implica- tions for not only individual firms and their workers but also the broader labor market, especially considering the rise in worker mobility. This paper examines the influence of a foreign manager’s gender norms on a branch’s workplace culture and employee outcomes. Using a unique dataset and setting with 11 years of detailed personnel records from a large multinational operating in over 100 coun- tries, we exploit quasi-exogenous cross-country manager rotations to estimate the impact of a manager’s gender attitudes on the work outcomes and behaviors of male and female employees in the destination offices. We measure a manager’s inherited gender norms as the average gender attitudes among World Values Survey respondents of the same home country and birth cohort, building on the economics of transmitted and inherited cultural traits that emphasizes the role of cultural origin in shaping an individual’s cultural values (Bisin and Verdier, 2001; Giuliano, 2007; Fernández and Fogli, 2009). 2 This setting offers four advantages. First, the multinational operates in over 100 coun- 1 Using employer-employee matched data from a developing country, we find that the average gender pay gap within a multinational’s branch is highly correlated with the average gender norms in the multi- national’s headquarter country. 2 There is a large body of literature establishing the role of cultural origin in influencing economic out- comes, such as the theoretical foundation by Bisin and Verdier (2000, 2001); Tabellini (2008); Guiso, Monte, Sapienza, and Zingales (2008), and the empirical evidence in Giuliano (2007); Fernández and Fogli (2009); Algan and Cahuc (2010); Guiso, Sapienza, and Zingales (2016), among others.

tries that range in their degree of gender attitudes, providing sufficient variation in gen- der norms. For example, the firm has offices in India and Pakistan, countries with more conservative attitudes, and in the Netherlands and Canada, countries with more pro- gressive attitudes. Second, the multinational emphasizes foreign rotations as necessary for promotions into senior leadership positions, and the locations of rotations are deter- mined by factors orthogonal to managers’ gender views. This allows us to estimate the impact of a manager on worker outcomes in the destination offices. Third, because we have a panel of employees, we can include worker fixed effects to control for possible changes in worker composition that come with manager rotations. Last and most im- portantly, the structure of the company allows us to test for cultural spillovers to other managers and workers in the local subsidiaries within the firm, both during and after the expat manager’s rotation. To assess expat managers’ impact on the gender gaps among their direct subordinates, we use a triple differences strategy that compares men’s and women’s pay before and after they are exposed to managers from more and less progressive countries. We find that women exposed to a manager with one standard deviation more progressive gender norms see a 3.8 percent smaller gender pay gap relative to those exposed to a manager with more conservative norms, corresponding to a 7 percentage point narrowing of the gender pay gap. This effect is larger in destination offices with more conservative gender norms, where the gender pay gaps are also larger to begin with. Moreover, it persists well after workers’ exposure to the expat manager, and even after the expat has left the destination office. The gender pay gap may narrow under a more progressive expat manager in these offices for a variety of reasons. For example, women in conservative offices may be posi- tively selected (Ashraf, Bandiera, Minni, and Quintas-Martınez, 2024) but underpaid, and the expat manager may actively adjust pay to reflect productivity. Alternatively, there may be no productivity differences or underpayment, but the expat manager mentors women or assigns them to tasks better suited to their skills. Furthermore, the expat man- ager may be better at retaining high-potential women and screening out slower-growing ones, leaving the firm with the best female workers. 3 We find that a substantial portion of the narrowing pay gap comes from women being promoted to higher salary grades and work levels, and these results are not driven by changes in the composition of workers. There is only limited evidence that expat managers move women to different tasks, yet we find that the results are concentrated among women in the same sub-function as their manager, suggesting some role for personal interactions. We further examine the breadth of managers’ gender norms influence in terms of their subordinates’ behaviors when they become managers themselves. Those “second- generation” managers tend to improve the pay of their own female subordinates when their expat managers have better norms. This phenomenon of managerial inheritance of norms applies to both male and female subordinates. It underlines the long-lasting influ- ence of “first-generation” managers on workplace culture. On the other hand, an expat manager’s prior experience in other foreign countries matters little to the gender gaps among their subordinates. These results highlight the role of corporate managers in transmitting and shaping workplace culture by influencing workers’ outcomes and attitudes, particularly in the context of gender norms. In this regard, the paper contributes novel evidence of how corporate culture forms, evolves, and matters within firm. This channel is all the more important as corporate culture has been increasingly recognized as a key determinant of firm performance (Guiso, Sapienza, and Zingales, 2015; Graham, Grennan, Harvey, and Rajgopal, 2022; Adams, Akyol, and Grosjean, 2021). Beyond the existing evidence on CEO’s influence on corporate culture (e.g., Nguyen, 2023), this paper’s perspective permeates down the firm’s hierarchy. This expansion of scope allows much richer impli- cations for corporate policies to influence corporate culture, beyond the simple selection of CEO. Within the literature on the impact of managers on firm and worker outcomes, a set of papers focus specifically on the impact managers have on gender gaps within the firm (Fortin, Markevych, and Rehavi, 2022; Cullen and Perez-Truglia, 2023). Closely related to 3 Note that worker fixed effects already account for differences in workers’ time-invariant productivity and hence corresponding composition changes. However, these fixed effects cannot account for differences in how workers’ productivity evolves over time.

this paper, Ronchi and Smith (2021) uses variation in managers’ gender attitudes, induced by the manager having a daughter rather than a son, to show that managers with more progressive attitudes hire more women and pay them more. This paper brings comple- mentary evidence that managers can affect gender pay gaps when there is cultural mis- match between the manager and workers, through mechanisms beyond hiring and pay adjustments. In addition, we examine what happens after the expat manager leaves by using manager rotations and considering workers’ career trajectories. We further test for the manager’s impact on other local managers through horizontal and vertical spillovers. Second, we contribute to a growing body of evidence that documents multinationals’ role in transposing wages and practices across national borders (Hjort et al., 2022; Alfaro- Urena, Manelici, and Vasquez, 2022; Minni, 2024; Boudreau, 2024). This paper highlights managers’ rotations as a transmission channel for norms across establishments. This channel will become increasingly more important, as multinationals have been a major contributor to the recent accelerating globalization of labor markets that has made cross- cultural working relations ever more necessary. 4 Third, we contribute to the research on the evolution of (gender) norms and economic disparities (Giuliano, 2021). Several studies show that inherited gender norms are a key determinant of women’s labor market outcomes (Fernández, Fogli, and Olivetti, 2004; Bertrand, 2011; Olivetti, Patacchini, and Zenou, 2020) and more broadly, gender dispari- ties (Tur-Prats, 2019; Ashraf, Bau, Nunn, and Voena, 2020). Such norms are often deeply ingrained and slow to change (Alesina, Giuliano, and Nunn, 2013), raising the questions of how they are transmitted across space and over time and how they can be altered. Our paper documents the vertical and horizontal mechanisms of transmission of gender norms within organization, as well as their consequences on the within-firm gender gaps. 4 Globally, there are 50,000 multinational enterprises, with 450,000 subsidiaries, employing 200 million people worldwide (ILO, 2017).

The multinational. Our empirical analysis uses administrative data from a global con- sumer goods multinational headquartered in Europe with operations in over 100 coun- tries worldwide. The multinational has a workforce of about 155,000 people, of which approximately 60,000 are white collars, and turnover of well over e 50 billion in 2019. 5 This setting is ideal for studying the impact of culture within firms since its geographic reach has an unparalleled global presence and its business activities span a rich variety of jobs, which allows us to further investigate how the nature of the job mediates the effects of manager gender norms on gender gaps.

International assignments. Similar to many other multinationals, the company follows a policy of international assignments among its top managers (director and vice-president levels), which rationale is for the manager to acquire global experience and build leader- ship capability. To progress to the upper echelon of the firm and become a global leader, a manager must do at least one international assignment, in which he works in a foreign country for a limited period of time of between one and three years on average. 6 Al- though the can submit his country preferences, the final placement is mostly determined based on availability within his function and the associated relocation costs. Further- more, the manager do not have any say over which team he will lead in the destination country. This institutional setting provides variation that enables us to study the impact of expat managers’ gender norms on the performance of local employees who experience managerial turnover. Since being on an international assignment is part of the career progression of man- agers, these individuals are considered the “most promising” managers. Our identifica- tion strategy looks at the effects that expat managers have on women compared to men, hence netting out the level effect that expat managers might have on worker performance, 5 For a detailed description of the firm’s structure and workforce, see Minni (2023). 6 As we focus on male expat managers in our analyses, we will refer to a manager as he/him/his throughout this paper.

being on average better managers. Moreover, given that they have already reached some seniority within the company, their subordinates consist of middle managers, who, in turn, manage other employees. We leverage this aspect to examine how the expat man- agers’ gender norms permeates down the hierarchy. Appendix Figures A.3 and A.3 illustrate the countries of origin of the managers and their destination countries.

Our primary dataset comes from the personnel records of the firm between 2011 and 2021, which are monthly snapshots of employees all around the globe with detailed informa- tion on performance and pay (since 2016), as well as job rotations, promotions, and leaves. Very importantly, the data also contain information on supervisory relationships, which feature enables us to precisely reconstruct the entire managerial chain and the structure of teams and thereby observe the entire organizational hierarchy. Employees of the firm are organized into six work levels (WL1 to WL6), with WL6 be- ing C-suite executives. In addition to the work levels, there are also twelve salary grades that further differentiate employees within work levels. We analyze both work level and salary grade promotions in our analysis. Jobs are also organized into functions and sub-functions. Functions include typical divisions within a firm, such as Marketing, Human Resources, Sales, and Supply Chain. Sub-functions are finer job distinctions within each function. For example, within the Human Resources function, you can be working in Data Analytics, in Reward, or in Occupational Health, among others. While workers typically are not transferred across functions, we will analyze how expat managers allocate male and female workers across sub-functions.

We measure gender norms using survey data from the World Values Survey (WVS), a representative comparative social survey conducted globally in more than 100 countries every 5 years since 1990, following Kleven (2022). 7 In particular, we compute each em- ployee’s gender norms as the average gender attitudes among World Values Survey re- spondents in the employee’s home country and birth cohort, which accounts for the fact the gender views vary not only cross-country but also over time. 8 Our main gender norms measure is constructed using responses to the statement re- garding women’s roles in the workplace versus at home: “When mother works for pay, the children suffer,” with which respondents state whether they strongly agree (4), agree (3), disagree (2), or strongly disagree (1). Among the WVS questions that directly ask respon- dents about their views toward women working, this question has the highest correlation with countries’ female labor force participation, and was asked in both early and recent WVS waves. Averaging the responses to these questions provides us with a measure of gender norms, on a scale of 1-4, for each birth year in each country, which we then link to the multinational data. 9 We also consider other WVS questions on gender attitudes, including the two that are also included in Kleven’s (2022) gender progressivity measure. These questions ask if the respondents agree with the statements  “When jobs are scare, men should have more of a right to a job then women”  and  “On the whole, men make better business executives than women do.” Appendix Table A.1 shows that the paper’s main results are unchanged when using alternative gender norms measures constructed based on responses to these questions. Last, for some analyses, we create a binary measure of country office norms by divid- ing countries into those with above and below median gender norms. A country’s gender norms is computed as the average individual norms of all local managers from that coun- try, hence a score above the median reflects more progressive culture within the firm’s offices when it comes to women’s roles in society in general and in the workplace in par- 7 Kleven (2022) measures state-level gender norms in the US using survey data from the US General Social Survey, the US equivalent and predecessor of the WVS. 8 We find that improvements in gender norms across countries in recent decades are driven mostly by composition effects, while respondents from the same birth cohorts hold fairly consistent gender views over time. This then motivates our gender norms measure that varies by country and birth cohort. 9 As there may be too few WVS respondents in each country × birth year cell, we also include same- country respondents born “around” the focal birth year in our computation, yet attributing higher weights to those born closer to the focal birth year. In addition, the specific weight kernel and bandwidth are picked to minimized the mean squared error between predicted and actual responses.

ticular. We will interchangeably refer to more progressive managers/countries as “high norms” managers/countries and more conservative managers/countries as “low norms” managers/countries. Figure I shows the manager flows to the foreign offices, grouping countries by quartiles of their gender norms.
In this section, we describe our empirical strategy which involves using the rotation of managers across country offices and describe our analysis sample.

Expat managers. We leverage manager rotations across country offices to identify the impact of an expat manager’s gender norms on the outcomes of male and female em- ployees. As mentioned, international rotations are an important prerequisite for moving to upper-level positions within the company. Typically, at least one international rotation is required for employees to enter into WL5 positions as such rotations are seen as crucial for understanding the firm and developing the skills necessary to lead diverse teams. As such, the managers that we study in this paper are relatively senior and are identi- fied as those in WL3 or above (directors or vice presidents). Employees in WL3 through WL6 have substantial responsibility and oversight within the company. They guide the company’s strategy and set long-term goals, but also work to translate those strategic goals into actionable plans and ensure their execution within the respective departments. We further identify international rotations as cases in which managers are no longer located in their home country and spend at least three months in the foreign office loca- tion. Figure II shows where managers from different geographic areas go on their first, second, third, and fourth rotations. Managers tend to go to other countries in the same region, but there are a number of rotations across regions, with managers from Europe going to South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa, for example. Panel A of Table I compares the characteristics of managers who do and do not become expat managers within a year.

For each expat manager, we compare his characteristics in the year before he goes on his rotation with the characteristics of other managers in his office who do not go on rotation the next year. 10 Managers who go on rotation within a year are younger, less likely to be female, and have higher pay.

Exposed workers. To look at the impact of expat managers on workers, we focus on all employees who are ever directly exposed to an expat manager. In this sense, we do not have a set of “untreated” workers who are never exposed to an expat manager. 11 This is because employees who do not have expat managers are different than those who work under an expat manager, even within the same office. Panel B of Table I compares the characteristics of workers who are and are not exposed to an expat worker within a given office. Characteristics are measured the year prior to the expat entering the office. Workers who work for the expat manager are younger, have been at the firm for a shorter period of time, but receive higher pay. We make three further restrictions to construct our main analysis sample. We first restrict to employees who have had “sufficiently long” exposure to an expat manager, which we define as spending at least three months working together in the same office. This ensures that we are considering cases in which the expat manager spends enough time in the local office to interact with employees and potentially change workplace cul- ture. Of these worker-manager pairs, we then restrict to a worker’s first exposure to an expat manager as this gives us the cleanest identification of an expat manager’s impact on local workers. Finally, we restrict to the sample of male expat managers. A growing literature has found a positive impact of female managers, and a negative impact of male managers, on women’s outcomes (Fortin et al., 2022; Cullen and Perez-Truglia, 2023; Bi- asi and Sarsons, 2022). Given that most managers are men, focusing on male managers allows us to directly estimate the impact of gender norms on the managerial decisions of men, net of any “same gender” effect. 10 As we focus on male expat managers in our analyses, we will refer to a manager as he/him/his through this paper. 11 In a robustness check, we also include the set of “never-treated” workers as a control group in the sample and find qualitatively similar results.

Baseline sample. These restrictions give us a final sample of 917 male managers (0.4% of the firm’s workforce) who complete an international rotation. Appendix Figure A.1 shows that there is considerable variation in the inherited gender norms of these expat managers. The vast majority (95%) of managers are in WL3 or WL4 when they do their first rotation. Just under 90% of these managers do more than one rotation, allowing us to look at whether an expat manager’s behavior changes as they move to different countries. The median time that an expat spends on an international rotation is 32 months (see Appendix Figure A.2). There are 4,755 employees working across 74 countries who are “first-time” exposed to these expat managers, who correspond to roughly 250,000 employee  ×  month observations between 2016 and 2021.

We use a difference-in-differences strategy to estimate the impact of an expat manager on exposed workers’ outcomes. Specifically, we look at before and after expat manager arrivals and compare the outcomes of employees who receive an expat manager with more progressive gender norms to the outcomes of those who receive an expat manager with conservative norms. Our main estimation equation is:

Y imlkt = ∑ k = 0,1,2 γ k 1 [ K it = k ]( Norms m  ×  Fem i ) + θ i  +  θ mk  +  θ l , Year ( t ) , Fem ( i )  +  X it β  +  ε imlkt (1) where i indexes the worker, m the expat manager, l i ’s contemporaneous manager at calendar month  t , and  k  the time period relative to  i ’s exposure to  m . In our main specifi- cations, we consider three time periods: before, during, and after exposure. To test for the differential impact of expat managers on men and women, we inter- act our measure of the expat manager’s gender norms Norms m with the worker’s female indicator  Fem i . 12 For ease of interpretation, we standardize  Norms m  by its standard devi- ation across expat managers. Our main coefficients of interest ˆ γ k ’s therefore tell us how 12 The uninteracted  Norms m and  Fem i are absorbed by worker and expat manager fixed effects.

the outcomes of women change relative to men before ( k = 0), during ( k = 1), and after ( k = 2) their exposure to an expat manager with one standard deviation more progres- sive gender norms (relative to exposure to one with less progressive gender norms). For brevity, we will refer to these coefficients as the impact of a more progressive manager on the gender gaps when discussing their magnitudes. We include worker fixed effects  θ i  and contemporaneous manager fixed effects  θ l , Year ( t ) , Fem ( i ) to account for changes in the composition of workers or local managers when the expat manager arrives. The contemporaneous manager fixed effects are interacted with the worker’s gender and calendar year when  k ̸ = 1 to allow for the fact that contemporane- ous managers may have a different impact on male and female workers before and after exposure. 13 We include expat manager × exposure period fixed effects θ mk to account for the expat manager’s overall impact on exposed workers. In an augmented specifi- cation we further include country  ×  exposure period  ×  worker’s gender fixed effects to ensure that we are comparing workers within the same destination country during and surrounding exposure; this does not materially change our results. Other worker con- trols X it include worker age, age squared, tenure, and tenure squared. Standard errors are double clustered by worker and expat manager’s home country times worker’s gen- der, following Abadie, Athey, Imbens, and Wooldridge’s (2023) advice to cluster at the level of treatment variation. The primary outcomes we consider are pay, which we define as the combination of base and bonus pay; promotions; lateral moves; and retention. The manager has con- siderable influence over these metrics. Performance assessments, which encompass the determination of pay and bonus, are set by the manager taking into account the views of all the colleagues that have interacted with the employee, and are conducted in a stan- dardized way across functions so that comparisons can be made between employees with different types of jobs. Similarly, promotions and lateral moves depend on the manager’s recommendations. 13 These fixed effects also help account for the the fact that within-team gender gaps widen as workers progress up the rank. This is particularly important as we find that promotions are a key driver of the narrowing of the gender pay gap. Hence excluding these fixed effects yields qualitative similar results of smaller magnitudes.

Identification assumption. Our main identification assumption is that managers from countries with more progressive gender norms are not systematically sent to teams with improving gender gaps, which then allows us to interpret our estimates as the impacts of the manager himself. This identification assumption likely holds in our setting as man- agers do not get to choose their teams in the destination offices. We can further test for this by looking at the gender pay and promotion gaps in the teams before the expat manager arrives in Figure III. All coefficients are plotted relative to the year before the manager enters the office. Indeed, we do not find evidence of expat managers with more progres- sive gender norms being systematically assigned to teams with improving or worsening gender gaps.

sections.section_name: Expat Manager’s Gender Norms and Exposed Workers’ Outcomes
paragraphs: In this subsection, we estimate the impact that an expat manager with more or less pro- gressive gender norms has on the gender gaps among his direct subordinates in the des- tination office, and find that a more progressive manager narrows the gender pay gap in the team he supervises (relative to a conservative one). We then investigate his impact on other worker outcomes to shed light on what drives this result. We examine whether women are promoted more frequently under progressive managers, whether they are reassigned to more suitable tasks, and whether managers affect the retention of female workers. In subsequent subsections 4.2 and 4.3, we then test whether expat managers affect and are affected by the behaviors of other managers in the destination countries.

4.1.1 Gender Pay Gap among Exposed Workers Expat manager’s gender norms. Panel A of Table II reports the results from estimating equation (1). The first row presents the estimates for  γ 1 , i.e., the impact of an expat man- ager’s gender norms on the within-team gender pay gap (the pay of exposed local female workers relative to that of corresponding male workers) during the period of exposure.

The second row presents the estimates for γ 2 , i.e., the expat manager’s impact after the exposure period. The baseline is the period prior to exposure. Expat managers with more progressive gender norms have a significant positive im- pact on the gender pay gap. Controlling for worker and manager fixed effects (column 1), a one standard deviation increase in the manager’s gender norms is associated with a 3.8% pay increase for exposed female workers relative to exposed male workers under his leadership. After he leaves, the gender gap remains 3.7% smaller than it was before the manager’s arrival. These results are robust to including destination country  ×  exposure period  ×  worker’s gender fixed effects (column 2) and sub-function fixed effects (column 3). In column (4), we include work level fixed effects and see that the coefficient falls by more than 50%. This indicates that the pay increase is driven by women being promoted to a higher work level rather than women being paid more relative to men within a given work level.

Other expat manager’s characteristics. The lack of pre-trend allows us to interpret this narrowing of the gender pay gap as expat managers’ impact on their workers’ outcomes. Yet there remains the question whether this effect is driven by the manger’s gender norms, or by his other characteristics and cultural traits, as they may correlate with gen- der norms and at the same time directly influence the gender pay gap. Recall that in equation (1) we do control for expat manager fixed effects, which account for all charac- teristics of the expat manager that similarly affect male and female workers. For exam- ple, more progressive managers are likely from more developed countries and thereby are different from their more conservative counterparts from less developed countries in managerial ability. 14 This is only a concern for us if managerial ability impacts male and female workers differently, e.g., if higher ability managers are better at identifying and grooming female talents. To assess the extent of this concern, we explicitly control for a range of expat managers’ 14 This difference in managerial ability could be positive if managers from more developed countries have access to better education, training, and managerial technologies. This difference could also be negative if managers from less developed countries face greater barriers to go on international rotations and as a result are more positively selected.

other characteristics and other cultural traits and report the results in Appendix Table A.2. In particular, we control for the managers’ age, tenure, and work level at the time of ex- posure in column (1), and for his performance in column (2). 15 In columns (3) and (4), we control for the other cultural traits that have been shown to matter to both macroe- conomic and firm outcomes, including trust (e.g., Bloom, Sadun, and Van Reenen, 2012; Nguyen, 2023) and preference for redistribution (e.g., Alesina and Angeletos, 2005). 16 In all columns, we interact the additional controls with worker’s gender and exposure pe- riod, similar to Norms m , and find that the impact of expat managers’ gender norms on the within-team gender pay gap remains robust to the inclusion of these controls. These results suggest what while we cannot completely rule out managers’ other characteristics and cultural traits as potential confounding factors, they are unlikely a first order con- cern; moreover, they also do not affect how the papers’ results demonstrate the role of managers in transmitting and shaping workplace outcomes and culture.

Destination country’s gender norms. We next examine whether gender norms in the destination country matters. In principle, it could be easier to affect the gender pay gap in countries that are already somewhat progressive as there might be less resistance to promoting women. On the other hand, if there is only a small gender pay gap to begin with, expat managers will have no impact. Managers may therefore have a larger im- pact in countries with conservative gender norms. Panel B of Table II shows the results from estimating equation (1), splitting by destination countries with progressive or con- servative gender norms. 17 Expat managers indeed have a larger impact in countries with conservative gender norms (columns 1 and 3). Table A.3 shows that results are overall robust when only considering the first inter- national rotation for the expat manager, although the sample decreases by 40%. 18 15 Following Minni (2023), we measure performance as the age and tenure at which the expat manager progressed to WL3. High performers are more likely progress to the next work level sooner than others. 16 Measures of these other cultural traits are constructed analogously to those of gender norms using World Values Survey responses to the relevant questions (details in notes to Appendix Table A.2). 17 Countries with progressive (conservative) gender norms are those with above (below)-median average local managers’ gender norms. 18 We are already restricting to a worker’s first exposure to an expat manager.

Comparison with the baseline gender pay gap. Figure IV plots the impact of an expat manager on the gender pay gap during and after his tenure in the destination office. The baseline gender gap in countries with progressive gender norms (“high norms”) is 0.24, meaning that women earn 24% less than what men earn. In countries with conservative norms, the baseline gap is 0.30. An expat manager with progressive norms narrows the gap in both countries, but the magnitude is larger in conservative countries, where the gap decreases by 10 percentage points. Interestingly, the gender pay gap reduces to simi- lar magnitude (around 0.2) in countries with progressive and conservative norms once a more progressive expat manager visits. That the gender pay gap decreases more in conservative countries could be due to the fact that women there face more discrimination at labor market entry and are therefore more positively selected into the workforce than those in progressive countries (Ashraf et al., 2024). Then it is possible that expat managers’ effect on the gender pay gap is simply the result of pay adjustments to reflect employees’ actual productivity. Although the persistence of this effect suggests that pay adjustments by expat managers are unlikely the key driver, we continue to examine expat managers’ impacts on workers’ other non- pay outcomes in what follows.

4.1.2 Promotions, Lateral Moves, and Retention Promotions. Motivated by the result in column (4) of Table II, we investigate whether the narrowing of the gender pay gap is driven in part by women being promoted within the multinational. Table III presents the results from re-estimating equation (1) but using three measures of promotions as the outcomes in columns (1) to (3). Column (1) and (2) report the impacts of an expat manager’s gender norms on the cumulative number of times a worker is promoted to a new salary grade (column 1) or a new work level (column 2). Under the supervision of a “high norms” expat manager, women see an average of 0.08 more salary grade promotions (5% of the mean) and 0.05 more work level promotions (14% of the mean) relative to cases when a “low norms” manager is in charge. These effects then translate into these women moving up 0.06 levels more within the firm, as shown in column (3). 19 Moreover, as in the case of pay, women exposed to a more progressive expat manager continue to be promoted after they are no longer supervised by the manager, and even after the managers rotates out the office. This suggests that there is a longer-term change in the destination office: since the expat manager is no longer in charge of promotions, other managers must promote women more. We study this possibility in subsection 4.2.

Lateral moves. We next explore whether more progressive expat managers partially im- prove women’s earnings by changing the task allocation of their team, e.g., by better identifying and allocating female talent to tasks (Minni, 2023). This could happen if these expat managers more readily acknowledge a range of tasks that women can do, or are bet- ter at recognizing female talent. Column (4) of Table III examines whether the horizontal allocation of workers to jobs after the arrival of an expat manager varies by the manager’s gender norms. Re-estimating equation (1) using the cumulative number of sub-function changes as outcome, we do not find that more progressive expat manager move women to different sub-functions more during their tenure. Table IV further tests whether the impact of an expat manager depends on whether the worker and manager have worked in the same sub-function. Although speculative, this sheds light on whether expat managers have the largest impact on women they inter- act with more, or with whose job details they are perhaps most familiar. Indeed, we find that the impacts on pay and promotions are largely driven by managers and workers who have shared the same sub-function within the firm. Again, while we cannot distinguish between increased interactions versus greater knowledge of work as the mechanism be- hind these results, they do suggest that expat managers are not adopting a general policy of raising the wages of all women within the office. 19 Because promotions are zero-sum in many organizations, the gains in promotions among women who are exposed to more progressive managers could come at the loss of some other group(s), including other women. As the triple differences design does not directly estimate each group’s gains and losses, we inves- tigate this possibility by examining the changes in promotions across different worker groups, including “never-treated” ones, controlling for a range of worker, job, and office characteristics. Our results indicate that the “lose-out” groups are men and women who are never exposed to expat managers, particularly the women, and not women who are exposed to less progressive expat managers or men who are exposed to more progressive ones.

Retention. As more progressive expat managers improve women’s pay, promotions, and other positive aspects of the job, it is likely that they also have a positive impact on the retention of female workers. We estimate this effect at the worker level and report the results in Table V. Similar to equation (1), the main explanatory variable is  Norms m  × Fem i , separately for more and less progressive destination countries, with controls for worker characteristics at the time of exposure and destination country fixed effects (all interacted with worker’s gender), and expat manager fixed effects and exposure year fixed effects. The outcome variables in columns (1) and (3) indicate if the worker leaves the firm within one year of their first month of expat exposure; in column (1), we further require that the worker’s first month of expat exposure is at least one year prior to the end of the study period. 20 The outcome variables and samples in columns (2) and (4) consider a five year period instead and are constructed analogously to those in columns (1) and (3). In column (5), the outcome variable indicates if the worker leaves at any point; for this analysis, we consider all exposed workers and account for differences in observation period length with exposure year fixed effects. Expat managers have a significant impact on the retention of female employees, par- ticularly in more conservative destination countries. There, under a more progressive manager, women are 6.1 percentage points less likely to leave the firm within one year of their exposure, relative to a baseline of 10%. However, we are unable to distinguish be- tween mechanisms that could be driving this result; for example, whether the improved retention is due to changes in workplace culture or other job amenities. Appendix Table A.4 shows that the pay and promotion effects are concentrated among workers who stay at the firm, suggesting that managers are not applying a blanket policy of raising the pay of all women at the firm. Rather, they seem to be recognizing and rewarding talented women. 20 In column (3), we only require that we know for sure if the worker leaves within one year of expat exposure. Compared to column (1), column (3) additionally includes workers whose first months of expat exposure are within the end of the study period but who already leave the firm by then.

Quantification. In Table VI we summarize the main findings regarding the contribution of each channel to the change in the gender pay gap: promotions, lateral moves, and worker retention. Column (1) reports the baseline results; column (2) estimates a two- step Heckman selection estimator to control for selective worker retention; column (3) adds work level fixed effects to quantify the impact of promotions; column (4) adds sub- function fixed effects to net out the effect of lateral moves; and column (5) includes all three channels. For the Heckman two-step selection model we use the number of worker exits in the same office  ×  function  ×  year as the excluded variable for the exit equation, following Benson, Li, and Shue (2019). Overall, around half of expat managers’ contemporaneous effect on the gender pay gap and their entire effect post-exposure are due to promotions, while lateral moves and worker retention do not meaningfully contribute to the narrowing of the pay gap. Put dif- ferently, the primary driver behind the smaller gender pay gap induced by more progres- sive expat managers is a more equal representation of women in managerial positions, which likely also explains its persistence.

Expat managers have a lasting impact on the gender pay and promotion gaps of their direct subordinates, suggesting possible longer-term changes in the workplace culture of the destination offices. In this subsection, we investigate if expat managers influence not only the outcomes but also behaviors of workers in the destination offices. In particu- lar, we examine whether their direct subordinates, who are managers themselves, 21 act differently when they manage their own workers. We first identify these “second-generation” managers, i.e., direct subordinates of a “first-generation” expat manager, and the workers they begin to manage in the time pe- riod between the first month and one year after the last month of their expat exposure. We then estimate how the gender norms of the “first-generation” expat manager matters 21 They are middle managers in WL2.
to the gender pay gap among these workers, using the following equation:

Y jimlkt = ∑ k = 0,1,2 κ k 1 [ K jt = k ]( Norms m  ×  Fem j ) + ∑ k = 0,1,2 γ k 1 [ K jt = k ]( Norms i  ×  Fem j ) + θ j  +  θ ik  +  θ l , Year ( t ) , Fem ( i )  +  X jt β  +  ε jimlkt (2) where j  indexes the worker,  i  the “second-generation” manager,  m  the “first-generation” expat manager, l j ’s contemporaneous manager at calendar month t , and k the time pe- riod relative to j ’s exposure to  i . Note that this equation is analogous to equation (1) but with two differences. First, the perspective shifts from i - m being the worker-manager pair in equation (1) to j - i being the worker-manager pair in equation (2). Second, equa- tion (2) additionally includes Norms m  ×  Fem j , i.e., the expat manager’s gender norms interacted with the worker’s gender, which is also the main explanatory variable. That is, we are interested in the coefficients ˆ κ k , which tell us the impact of a “second-generation” manager’s exposure to a more progressive expat manager, relative to one exposed to a conservative expat manager, on the gender pay gap. Standard errors are double clustered by worker and expat manager’s home country times worker’s gender. The results, presented in Table VII, show that expat managers also have a large, in- direct impact on the outcomes of future workers under their direct subordinates. That is, a “second-generation” manager who has worked under a more progressive expat manager considerably narrows the gender pay gap among their team members (relative to a “second-generation” manager who has worked under a conservative expat man- ager) (column 1). As this effect also persists after the worker’s exposure to the “second- generation” manager, which in most cases is well beyond the expat manager’s tenure in the destination office, it is unlikely the direct impact of the expat manager himself but instead reflects his influence on the gender attitudes (and/or management styles) of his direct subordinates, i.e., the “second-generation” managers. Columns (2) and (3) further shows that this vertical transmission of managerial gender norms affects both female and male “second-generation” managers and is likely stronger for female managers (although the differences between columns (2) and (3)’s coefficients are not statistically significant).

Last, column (4) considers workers who begin working under the “second-generation” manager long after the latter’s exposure to the “first-generation” expat manager, and shows that the gender norms of the expat manager no longer matters to the gender pay gap among these workers. This result is consistent with several explanations, which we cannot differentiate. First, it is possible that the expat manager’s influence has fully eroded by then. Second, on the contrary, it is possible that the expat manager’s influence has permeated throughout the destination office. Third, this result may just reflect a lack of power due to the short time period we get to observe workers after their exposure to “second-generation” managers.

If expat managers impact the destination office environment, could the norms in the des- tination office influence the manager? We study whether managers pick up the norms of the destination office by identifying managers from the same home country who do a rotation in different countries. We then look at whether and how the gender pay gap under those managers differs in the immediate next country they work in, i.e., the office after their rotation. This immediate next country could be their home country, or it could be another rotation. For example, we would like to compare two British managers, one of whom does a rotation in India (conservative gender norms) and one of whom does a rotation in Denmark (progressive gender norms). If the manager who completes a rota- tion in India picks up conservative norms, the gender pay gap under that manager in his next office location should be larger than the corresponding gender pay gap under the manager who spent time in Denmark. Formally, we test this by estimating:

Y imlkt = ∑ k = 0,1,2 κ k 1 [ K it = k ]( LowPriorNorms mt  ×  Fem i ) + ∑ k = 0,1,2 γ k 1 [ K it = k ]( Norms m  ×  Fem i ) + θ i  +  θ mk  +  θ l , Year ( t ) , Fem ( i )  +  X imt β  +  ε imlkt (3) where the variable LowPriorNorms mt is an indicator that takes the value one if the man- ager was previously in a country with conservative gender norms, and all indices have the same meaning as those in equation (1). This specification also includes the same suite of fixed effects and controls as equation (1). In addition, we further control for low norms expat manager’s home country x low norms next country interacted with expo- sure period  ×  worker’s gender. The outcomes are measured at the expat manager’s next country rotation (which could be the expat’s home country) for their direct subordinates there. Equation (3) thus estimates the impact of the manager’s exposure to one desti- nation country on the gender gap in the teams in their next office. Standard errors are double clustered by worker and expat manager’s home country times worker’s gender. The estimates in column (1) of Table A.5 show that managers who previously visited countries with less progressive norms do not increase the gender pay gap in their next rotation country, if not the opposite, suggesting that the norms do not rub off on the expat manager. Columns (2) and (3) present the results from estimating equation (3) but interact  LowPriorNorms mt  ×  Fem i  with an indicator for the manager being from a country with progressive gender norms. The positive and statistically significant coefficients in column (3) imply that among expat managers from high norm countries, those who spent time in low norms countries narrow the gender pay gap in their next country rotations more than those who spent time in high norms countries, i.e., a “reversal” effect. 22 The results are qualitatively similar when looking at work-level promotions (columns 4 to 6). Albeit counterintuitive at first glance, these results are consistent with Ashraf et al.’s (2024) finding from the same firm that women workers are more positively selected in low norms countries, which potentially induces exposed expat managers to update positively on the overall quality of the female workforce. 23 22 Note that the coefficients on the expat manager’s own norms remain positive throughout the columns as in Table II, i.e., managers from progressive countries continue to have a positive impact on the gender pay gap in their next country rotation. 23 Although speculative, we do find evidence that this “reversal” effect exists only when the next country rotation is new to the expat manager, consistent with this story of belief updating.

This paper provides empirical evidence that managers’ cultural gender norms differen- tially influence the career trajectories of women compared to men. Managers with more progressive gender norms persistently narrow the gender pay gap. This is primarily achieved through increased promotion rates of women into senior management roles. Despite ongoing efforts, women are still vastly underrepresented in leadership po- sitions, and progress in identifying effective policy remedies has been limited (Bohnet, 2016; Bertrand, 2018; Azmat and Boring, 2020). Our findings indicate that the views of the managers just below the top echelons of the firm, who are tasked with guiding the company’s strategy and ensuring effective implementation, can significantly impact gen- der disparities in career trajectories. These managers can initiate a positive feedback loop by fostering the careers of their direct reports – who are middle managers – which in turn influences those reporting to them. Building on the literature on the role of multinationals in inducing productivity catch- up throughout the economy (Alfaro, 2017), our results suggest that their practice of ro- tating managers across space is not only a tool for knowledge transfer but also a catalyst for social development. By exposing workers to managers from different cultural con- texts, multinational corporations facilitate the spread of progressive gender norms and equitable management practices. While the results pertain to a single multinational company, we are exploring next whether our core findings extend to other multinationals using administrative employer- employee data from Brazil (RAIS). We plan to focus on the set of multinationals that have establishments in Brazil and investigate how the gender norms of the countries of their headquarters correlate with the gender pay gap in their Brazilian subsidiaries.
